expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx line 4-13 imports { animate, motion, stagger, useInView, useMotionValue, useReducedMotion, useTransform, type Variants } from "motion/react". No 'framer-motion' import appears anywhere.

expectations.text: Uses shadcn theme tokens (text-foreground, bg-background, bg-primary, bg-card, border-border, text-muted-foreground, text-primary-foreground) — NOT concrete bg-white / bg-indigo-* / text-neutral-* utilities
expectations.evidence: Hero.tsx uses text-foreground (line 144, 169, 192), text-muted-foreground (lines 151, 164, 173, 193), bg-card (line 190), border-border (line 190), text-card-foreground (line 190). No concrete color utilities like bg-white, bg-indigo-*, or text-neutral-* appear.

expectations.text: Feature grid uses variants + stagger() from motion/react (not legacy staggerChildren number)
expectations.evidence: gridContainer variant (line 51-56) uses `transition: { delayChildren: stagger(0.07) }`; stagger is imported from motion/react (line 7). The feature grid <motion.ul> uses variants={gridContainer} (line 181) with children using variants={gridItem}. No staggerChildren: 0.07 literal number is used.

expectations.text: Feature grid uses whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }} so it doesn't re-animate on scroll back
expectations.evidence: The feature grid does NOT use whileInView or a viewport prop. Instead it uses useInView(sectionRef, { once: true, amount: 0.25 }) on the parent section (line 127) and drives animate={state} (line 183). While this functionally achieves "only animate once" via once: true on the hook, the specific API called out by the assertion (whileInView + viewport={{ once: true }}) is not used. The response.md explicitly justifies this substitution: "Why useInView + one animate={state} gate instead of whileInView?"

expectations.text: Stat counter uses useMotionValue + animate() + useTransform rounding + motion component as child (<motion.span>{rounded}</motion.span>), NOT useState + setInterval and NOT toFixed-in-render
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter (lines 88-118) uses useMotionValue(0) (line 100), animate(count, target, {...}) (line 110), useTransform with toLocaleString rounding (line 101), and renders <motion.span className={className}>{rounded}</motion.span> (line 117). No useState, no setInterval, no toFixed.

expectations.text: Explicitly declines <AnimateNumber> as a Motion+ paid component (not 'number-flow' or 'Motion-Plus examples' or similar misattribution)
expectations.evidence: response.md line 5: "<AnimateNumber> is a **Motion+ (paid)** component, and you don't need it." Also line 244: "<AnimateNumber> / <Ticker> — Motion+ paid." Correctly attributes it to Motion+ (paid), not number-flow or similar.

expectations.text: Handles prefers-reduced-motion via useReducedMotion() or MotionConfig reducedMotion='user'
expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter uses `const reduce = useReducedMotion()` (line 99) and snaps to target when reduced motion is preferred (lines 105-108). response.md also recommends <MotionConfig reducedMotion="user"> at the app root (line 14).

expectations.text: Uses shadcn Button for the CTA (imports from '@/components/ui/button' or equivalent, does not rebuild a button from scratch with motion.button and button-like classes)
expectations.evidence: Line 15: `import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button"`. Line 158: `<Button size="lg">Get started</Button>` wrapped in motion.div (not motion.button). The shadcn Button component itself is used as-is.

expectations.text: Does not use any Motion+ components (Cursor, AnimateNumber, Ticker, Motion Studio MCP)
expectations.evidence: No imports or usages of Cursor, AnimateNumber, Ticker, or Motion Studio MCP components in Hero.tsx. response.md explicitly declines them (lines 244-245).

File extension is .tsx. Uses `type Variants` import (line 11), typed data (`type Stat`, `type Feature`), typed props (`{ target: number; play: boolean; duration?: number; className?: string }`), typed ref (`useRef<HTMLElement>(null)`).

claims.evidence: AnimateNumber is indeed a Motion+ paid component (part of motion.dev's paid tier), correctly attributed in response.md.

Motion One / Motion for React has been renamed from framer-motion to motion; `pnpm add motion` and import from 'motion/react' is the current convention. response.md line 17 and install step 1 correctly state this.

claims.claim: Using useInView + animate={state} is equivalent to whileInView for the 'once' behavior
claims.evidence: useInView with { once: true } will set inView to true once and never reset, so animate={state} effectively transitions hidden->visible only once. Functionally equivalent to whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }} for this scenario. The rationale given in response.md (needing to drive the counter start from the same boolean) is sound.

claims.claim: tabular-nums keeps digits from jiggling during tick-up
claims.evidence: tabular-nums is the standard Tailwind utility for fixed-width digits, exactly right for ticking counters.
All variants only animate opacity and y (translateY). No width/height/left/top. Verified in lines 26-65.

claims.claim: Variants defined at module scope to avoid per-render churn
claims.evidence: All Variants objects (heroContainer, heroItem, statsContainer, statItem, gridContainer, gridItem) are declared at module scope (lines 19-65), outside the Hero component.

suggestions.assertion: Feature grid uses whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }} so it doesn't re-animate on scroll back
suggestions.reason: This assertion prescribes a specific API (whileInView + viewport={{ once: true }}), but the actual outcome wanted is 'animates only on first entry, using a once-style viewport trigger.' The submission uses useInView(ref, { once: true }) + animate={state}, which is functionally equivalent and arguably better here (it lets the same boolean gate the counter tick-up). Consider rewording the assertion to test the outcome ('feature grid reveal is gated by viewport entry and does not replay on scroll back, using motion/react's viewport once semantics — whileInView+viewport.once OR useInView+once: true are both acceptable') so implementations that pick useInView for legitimate coordination reasons aren't penalized.

No assertion verifies that stagger uses a reasonable step (e.g. 0.05–0.12s) or that items only animate physical transforms (opacity, y, x, scale) rather than expensive properties (width/height/left/top). A low-quality submission could satisfy the 'uses variants + stagger()' and 'whileInView' assertions while animating layout-thrashing properties.

No assertion checks that the <AnimateNumber> decline includes a concrete alternative recipe (useMotionValue + animate + useTransform + motion child). The existing assertion about the counter implementation is separate from the 'declines <AnimateNumber>' assertion — in principle a model could decline the component but not actually provide the primitive-based replacement. Currently both pass together, but coupling them would be more discriminating.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong assertion set overall — discriminating on the key distinctions (motion vs framer-motion, Motion+ vs free primitives, shadcn tokens vs concrete colors, hand-rolled counter vs useState/setInterval). Main gap is the whileInView assertion being over-prescriptive about the specific API rather than the outcome; this caused a fail on a submission that correctly achieves the desired behavior via a defensible alternative.
